quote: Is there anybody else on the title of the home with you?
stewart_read: Holland asks the title question correctly and learns that Zoe Pitts is the owner of record. Jacob clarifies he is on the deed but Zoe's name is on the loan. Holland acknowledges and moves on without addressing the spouse-inclusion implication. Per schema, a co-owner on the title must be included in the appointment. Holland captured the fact but did not act on it — no attempt to include Zoe in the scheduling conversation.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland may have been planning to address Zoe's inclusion at button-up, but the call never reached a formal button-up — so the deferral became a miss by default.
coaching_implication: Manager should replay 02:05-02:29 with Holland. The correct move after learning Zoe is on the title is to immediately flag her as a required participant: 'We'll want Zoe on the call too — what time works for both of you?' Holland captured the data but did not execute the protocol. Per schema protocols.spouse_decision, this is a procedure gap, not a persuasion gap.

quote: how how much would you say you guys are spending on average a month on that bill?
stewart_read: Holland captures the bill range ($200-$400) and acknowledges it ('400 is quite expensive') but immediately pivots to the credit qualifier without flipping the bill into emotional alignment against the utility. This is the universal corpus failure pattern: ask + acknowledge + move on. The flip was available — Jacob's $400 ceiling is real pain that could have been turned into the emotional engine of the pitch.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read holds. There is no evidence Holland was setting up a later flip — the call ends without one.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to introduce the objection_inversion technique per schema coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'Yeah, $400 a month — that's the bill solar replaces. That's the number we're working against.' Holland acknowledged the pain but left it on the table. This is the floor's universal coaching priority and this call is a clean example of the gap.

quote: So well, one of them is the big one on the left side of the house is gone. The one on the right side like, if you're looking at the house, the one on the right side, we we trimmed down, like, extreme.
stewart_read: Jacob is describing active shade remediation — a large tree removed, a second tree trimmed 'extreme.' This is a customer who has already taken physical action to improve solar viability on his property. That is a strong qualification signal and a buying-intent indicator. Holland's response ('Okay. That's cool. Alright.') acknowledges but does not anchor on the signal.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have read this as routine verify data rather than a buying-intent signal — reactive read holds.
coaching_implication: Manager should highlight this moment as a missed anchor opportunity. Jacob removing trees is not a neutral fact — it signals he has already invested in making solar work. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'Wait — you actually took down a tree for this? That tells me you're serious about making this work.' Anchoring on the action elevates Jacob's own commitment in his mind and builds momentum toward the appointment.

quote: he said you guys have the ability to move it as well, which I thought was pretty unique and worth at least a conversation.
stewart_read: Jacob is volunteering his specific interest hook — the portability feature — unprompted. This is the call's clearest buying signal and the natural bridge to the appointment: 'That's exactly what our specialist walks through — how the system moves with you if you ever relocate. Let's get you on the calendar.' Holland instead pivots to a feature explanation ('we're a fully in house company') and then to bill collection, walking past the appointment bridge.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland's feature response is legitimacy-building, not scope creep — the alternative hypothesis is that she was warming Jacob before the close. But the call never reaches a close, so the deferral becomes a miss.
coaching_implication: Manager should replay 03:44-04:14 with Holland. Jacob handed her the appointment bridge on a referral-validated platter. The correct move is to reflect his interest back and pivot immediately to scheduling: 'That's exactly what the specialist covers — let's get you on the calendar so you can see the full picture.' Instead, Holland explained features and then moved to bill collection, losing the momentum of the call's energy peak.

quote: Yeah. We're we're also a fully in house company, which makes it nice because we don't go through any third parties or anything. But, yeah, if you guys ever did need, like, a reroof down the road as well, we can lift those panels up, replace the roof, and then put them back as well.
stewart_read: Holland's roofing mention here is a responsive legitimacy move — Jacob had just cited the portability feature as his interest hook, and Holland is reinforcing Ion's in-house capability. Jacob has not indicated a current roofing need, so this is not a missed cross-sell. The mention is appropriate in context. The stronger read is that this is a passive feature mention that lands well but is not the highest-leverage use of the moment — the appointment bridge was available and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: This could be read as mild scope creep (setter explaining company operations). But the content is brief, responsive, and does not derail the call — the reactive read of scope creep is not well-supported here.
coaching_implication: No correction needed on the roofing mention itself. Manager should note that Holland's instinct to reinforce legitimacy here was sound, but the moment immediately after Jacob's portability comment was the appointment bridge — not a feature explanation. The coaching priority is what Holland did NOT do (pivot to scheduling) rather than what she did.

quote: It's in the mail. And I'm driving home from Tennessee right now, so I'll be home around 03:00 EST.
stewart_read: Jacob volunteers a specific availability window (3pm EST) and a clear reason for the delay (driving, bill is paper). This is not a deflection — it is a customer scheduling himself. Holland had everything she needed to lock a follow-up time or a tentative appointment slot. Instead she accepted a soft callback ('send that whenever you get home') with no time anchor, no appointment, and no button-up protocol executed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Holland may have judged that pushing for a locked time while Jacob is driving would feel pressuring. But the schema button_up protocol requires a time anchor precisely because vague callbacks have high no-show rates — the reactive read holds.
coaching_implication: Manager should replay 04:31-04:46 with Holland. Jacob gave her a 3pm EST window. The correct move is: 'Perfect — I'll text you right at 3. And while I have you, let's go ahead and get you and Zoe on the calendar for the specialist call — what does your week look like after 3 today?' Holland left a locked appointment on the table. Per schema button_up, the verbal handshake and time anchor are predictive of show rate — skipping them on a warm referral lead is a paycheck-protection gap.

quote: As long as Zoe has a credit score above 670, then you guys will qualify.
stewart_read: Holland correctly identifies Zoe as the title owner and routes the credit qualifier to her — this is accurate and appropriate given the title situation. However, Holland asks Jacob whether Zoe qualifies ('Is that the case for you guys?') and accepts Jacob's 'Yeah' as confirmation. Jacob is not Zoe. The credit qualifier is being confirmed by the wrong person. This is a soft protocol gap — the qualifier was asked, but the answer is unverifiable through Jacob.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that Jacob would know Zoe's credit situation given they are engaged and bought the home together. This is plausible but not confirmed in the transcript.
coaching_implication: Manager should flag this as a closer-protection issue. If Zoe's credit does not meet the threshold, the sit disqualifies at proposal — and Holland's confirmation came from Jacob, not Zoe. The correct move is to note the uncertainty: 'Great — and Zoe will be on the call with us so the specialist can confirm everything with her directly.' This also doubles as the Zoe-inclusion bridge per protocols.spouse_decision.

quote: Yeah. That would be amazing. I'll just send you a text then, and then you can just send that whenever you get home.
stewart_read: This is the call's closing line and it is a soft callback, not a booked appointment. No time anchor, no appointment slot, no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor, no Zoe inclusion. The button_up protocol is entirely absent. Holland's energy throughout the call was warm and competent, but the close did not convert the call's built energy into a sit.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that booking the appointment before the bill is captured is premature. Lead type metadata would resolve this — if organic/referral, booking without the bill is permissible per schema context.lead_types_and_bill_collection_rules.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this as the primary coaching moment for button_up protocol. Holland had a warm, referral-primed customer with a specific availability window and a stated interest hook. The full button_up sequence was available. Suggested coaching frame: 'What would it have taken to put Zoe and Jacob on the calendar before you hung up?' Walk Holland through the protocol elements she skipped and connect each one to show-rate and paycheck protection.
